name: NAFTA/USMCA Certificate of Origin
summary: Drafts a legally compliant NAFTA/USMCA Certificate of Origin to certify that goods qualify for preferential tariff treatment under the USMCA or legacy NAFTA. This skill gathers shipment details, verifies rules of origin, and completes the certification for exporters, producers, or importers. Use it for international trade documentation between the US, Mexico, and Canada to enable reduced customs duties.
content:
# NAFTA/USMCA Certificate of Origin - Professional Legal Workflow

You are tasked with preparing a legally compliant NAFTA/USMCA Certificate of Origin, a critical regulatory document that enables goods to qualify for preferential tariff treatment under the United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement (USMCA, which replaced NAFTA on July 1, 2020) or under the legacy North American Free Trade Agreement for applicable transactions predating the transition. This certificate serves as a formal legal declaration that the goods being exported meet the rules of origin requirements established by the trade agreement and are therefore eligible for reduced or eliminated customs duties when imported into a member country.

## Document Research and Information Gathering

Begin by conducting a comprehensive review of all available documentation related to the shipment, production, and parties involved in this transaction. Search through any uploaded documents, contracts, commercial invoices, bills of materials, production records, and prior certificates to extract accurate information about the exporter, producer, importer, and the goods themselves. Pay particular attention to identifying the legal business names exactly as they appear in official registrations, complete addresses with all geographic identifiers, tax identification numbers, business registration numbers, and any prior origin determinations or tariff classifications. If multiple producers are involved or if the supply chain includes components from various sources, trace the production history to determine the appropriate origin criterion and ensure all non-originating materials undergo the required substantial transformation or meet the applicable regional value content requirements.

Verify the effective date of the applicable agreement for this transaction, as the rules of origin, certification requirements, and available criteria differ between NAFTA and USMCA. For shipments on or after July 1, 2020, USMCA provisions govern unless specific transition rules apply. Confirm whether this certificate will be used for a single shipment or will serve as a blanket certificate covering multiple shipments of identical goods over a specified period not exceeding twelve months. Review any industry-specific rules of origin that may apply to the goods in question, particularly for automotive products, textiles and apparel, agricultural goods, or other sectors with special requirements under the agreement.

## Party Identification and Certification Authority

Document complete and legally accurate information for the exporter, beginning with the full legal business name exactly as registered with relevant governmental authorities in the country of export. Include the complete physical address with street number and name, city, state or province, postal code, and country, ensuring consistency with official business registration documents. Provide all applicable identification numbers including tax identification numbers, business registration numbers, and any customs-assigned importer or exporter codes. If you are the exporter and also the producer of the goods, clearly indicate this relationship by stating "Same as above" or "Same" in the producer field, as this affects the knowledge requirements for certification.

For the producer of the goods, provide identical detail including the full legal name, complete address with all geographic identifiers, and relevant identification numbers. When multiple producers are involved in manufacturing the goods or when components are sourced from various facilities, you must either identify each producer separately with complete information or, if confidentiality is required and permitted under USMCA Article 5.2 or equivalent NAFTA provisions, you may state "Available to Customs upon request" in the producer field. Be aware that choosing confidentiality may trigger additional verification procedures by customs authorities and you must be prepared to provide the actual producer information upon official request within thirty days.

Document the importer of record who will be receiving the goods in the destination country, including their full legal business name exactly as it appears on business registration or tax documentation, complete physical address with all necessary geographic identifiers, and any relevant tax identification or importer numbers required by the destination country's customs authority. If the importer is unknown at the time of certification because the goods will be sold after export or the ultimate consignee has not been determined, you may indicate "Unknown," "To Whom It May Concern," or "Various" in the importer field, though be aware that some customs authorities may require this information to be updated before granting preferential treatment, and the importer will need to possess a valid certificate at the time of claiming preferential treatment.

## Goods Description, Classification, and Origin Determination

Provide a detailed and precise description of the goods being certified that allows customs officials to identify and verify the products without ambiguity or the need for physical examination. The description should include the commercial name by which the goods are known in trade, technical specifications such as model numbers or part numbers, material composition identifying the primary materials from which the goods are made, dimensions or capacity where relevant, intended use or function, and any distinguishing characteristics that differentiate these goods from similar products. Avoid vague or generic descriptions such as "parts" or "merchandise" that could apply to numerous different products.

Assign the appropriate Harmonized System (HS) tariff classification number at the six-digit level minimum, which is the internationally standardized classification system used by customs authorities worldwide. Depending on the importing country's requirements and the specificity needed to identify the applicable rule of origin, you may need to provide the eight-digit or ten-digit classification used in that country's tariff schedule. Ensure the HS classification accurately reflects the goods in their condition as they will be imported, not as they existed at an intermediate stage of production, and verify that this classification matches what will be declared to customs authorities on the commercial invoice and entry documentation.

Determine and specify the applicable origin criterion that qualifies the goods for preferential treatment under the agreement, conducting a thorough analysis of the production process and material sourcing. Under USMCA, select the appropriate criterion from the following: Criterion A indicates the good is wholly obtained or produced entirely in the territory of one or more USMCA countries, meaning all materials and components originate within the United States, Mexico, or Canada with no third-country content; Criterion B indicates the good is produced entirely in the territory of one or more USMCA countries exclusively from originating materials, meaning that while the good underwent production in the USMCA region, all of its inputs have already qualified as originating; Criterion C indicates the good is produced entirely in the territory of one or more USMCA countries using non-originating materials that nonetheless satisfy the applicable rule of origin through sufficient transformation, regional value content, or other requirements specified in the agreement's product-specific rules; or Criterion D applies specifically to certain automotive goods that meet special requirements under USMCA's enhanced automotive rules of origin.

For legacy NAFTA certificates applicable to pre-USMCA transactions, apply the corresponding NAFTA criteria which follow a similar but not identical structure, and ensure you reference the correct agreement framework based on the date of export and the applicable transition provisions. Calculate any required regional value content using the appropriate methodology specified in the agreement, whether net cost method or transaction value method, and maintain detailed worksheets and supporting documentation for this calculation. Verify that any required tariff shift has occurred by confirming that non-originating materials are classified in a different tariff heading or subheading than the finished good, as specified in the product-specific rules of origin.

## Legal Certification Statement and Compliance Declarations

Draft a comprehensive certification statement that constitutes a formal legal declaration under penalty of applicable laws regarding false statements to government authorities, including potential criminal prosecution under 18 U.S.C. § 1001 in the United States or equivalent provisions in Mexico and Canada. The certification must affirm that you have examined the goods or have knowledge of the facts surrounding their production and origin through your role in the company, access to production records, or other reliable means of verification. State unequivocally that the goods described in this certificate qualify as originating goods under the rules of origin set forth in Chapter 4 (Rules of Origin) and Chapter 5 (Origin Procedures) of the USMCA, or Chapter 4 and Chapter 5 of NAFTA for applicable legacy transactions, and that all information provided in this certificate is true, accurate, and complete to the best of your knowledge and belief.

Include specific reference to the legal authority under which you are making this certification, acknowledging that this certificate is provided voluntarily to enable the importer to claim preferential tariff treatment and that you understand the importer will rely on this certification when making entry with customs authorities. State your understanding that this document may be subject to verification by customs authorities of any USMCA party country through written requests for information, questionnaires, verification visits to your facilities, or other means provided for in the agreement, and that you are required to maintain all records and supporting documentation that substantiate this certification for a minimum period of five years from the date of signature for USMCA certifications, or five years from the date of importation for NAFTA certifications.

Affirm your commitment to provide such documentation to customs authorities upon request within thirty days or such other period as may be specified in the verification request, and acknowledge that failure to respond adequately to verification requests may result in denial of preferential tariff treatment for the certified goods. Include a statement affirming your awareness that making false statements, providing fraudulent documentation, or certifying goods that do not qualify for preferential treatment may result in civil penalties up to the domestic value of the merchandise for each violation, criminal prosecution under applicable customs and fraud statutes, loss of preferential tariff treatment requiring payment of duties at the most-favored-nation rate plus interest, suspension or revocation of customs privileges, and other legal consequences under the customs laws of the importing country.

## Execution, Period Coverage, and Authorized Signature

Specify whether this certificate applies to a single importation of the described goods or serves as a blanket certificate covering multiple shipments of identical goods that will be imported during a specified period. For blanket certificates, clearly state the period of coverage with specific start and end dates, ensuring the period does not exceed twelve months from the date of signature as required by USMCA Article 5.2 and NAFTA Article 501. Indicate that the importer must be in possession of this certificate at the time of making each claim for preferential treatment during the blanket period, and that any changes to the goods, production process, or material sourcing that would affect origin qualification must be communicated immediately and may require a new certification.

Conclude with the signature of an authorized representative who has direct knowledge of the facts surrounding the origin of the goods or who has responsibility for ensuring the accuracy of origin determinations within the certifying company. The signatory must be legally empowered to bind the certifying party to the declarations made in this certificate, whether as an officer, director, partner, or employee with delegated authority for origin certifications. Include the printed name of the signatory exactly as it appears in company records, their title or position within the organization, the complete legal name of the company on whose behalf they are signing, the date of signature in a clear format indicating day, month, and year, and complete contact information including direct telephone number with country and area codes and email address for correspondence with customs authorities.

Ensure the signatory has actual authority to execute this certification on behalf of the exporter or producer, as unauthorized signatures may invalidate the certificate and expose both the individual and the company to liability for improper certification. If the certification is being completed by the exporter based on information provided by the producer, ensure you have received a written statement or certification from the producer affirming the origin of the goods, and maintain this supporting documentation with your records. Review the completed certificate for accuracy, consistency, and completeness before signature, as amendments or corrections after the fact may raise questions about the reliability of the certification and could trigger enhanced scrutiny from customs authorities.

## Output Format and Documentation Requirements

Generate the completed Certificate of Origin in a clear, professional format that includes all required data elements in the sequence specified by the applicable agreement and customs authorities. Present the information in a structured document with clearly labeled fields for exporter, producer, importer, description of goods, HS classification, origin criterion, certification statement, and signature block. Ensure all text is legible, all required fields are completed with "Unknown" or "N/A" indicated where information is legitimately unavailable, and the document is suitable for submission to customs authorities in either paper or electronic format as accepted by the importing country.

Include a cover memorandum or guidance notes that explain the basis for the origin determination, identify any supporting documentation that should be maintained in the company's records, highlight any areas of uncertainty or risk that may warrant legal review, and provide recommendations for ongoing compliance monitoring. Flag any situations where the origin qualification is marginal, where regional value content calculations are close to the minimum threshold, where the interpretation of product-specific rules is unclear, or where changes in sourcing or production methods could affect future origin determinations. Ensure the final document package provides a complete record that will support the certification if subject to customs verification and demonstrates the exercise of reasonable care in making the origin determination.
